questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-2,xmax=5,ymin=-2,ymax=6,xtick={-2,-1,...,5},ytick={-2,-1,...,6},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-2:4,samples=2]{x+2} node[pos=0.12,left,text=blue!70!black]{$y=x+2$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The graph of $y=x+2$ is shown. Use it to solve $x+2=5$.

options.why: Read off the $y$-value $5$ instead of the $x$-coordinate at the intersection.
Read the intersection one gridline too far right, at $(4,6)$.
Added the target $5$ and the intercept $2$ instead of reading the graph.

questions.solution_text:
Draw the horizontal line $y=5$.
It meets $y=x+2$ at $(3,5)$.
$x=3$

questions.question_text: To solve $3x+4=10$ using a graph, which two lines should you draw?

options.why: Drew a vertical line $x=10$ instead of the horizontal line $y=10$.
Dropped the constant $+4$ from the line's equation.
Swapped the constant and the target between the two lines.

questions.solution_text:
$3x+4=10$ has the form $mx+c=k$.
Draw the line $y=3x+4$ and the horizontal line $y=10$.

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=5,ymin=-4,ymax=2,xtick={-1,0,...,5},ytick={-4,-3,...,2},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-1:5,samples=2]{x-3} node[pos=0.12,left,text=blue!70!black]{$y=x-3$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The graph of $y=x-3$ is shown. Use it to solve $x-3=0$.

options.why: Read the $y$-intercept $-3$ instead of the $x$-intercept where the line meets the $x$-axis.
Read the $x$-value at the $y$-axis rather than where the line crosses the $x$-axis.

questions.solution_text:
$y=0$ is the $x$-axis, so read the $x$-intercept.
The line meets the $x$-axis at $(3,0)$.
$x=3$

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-4,xmax=3,ymin=-4,ymax=5,xtick={-4,-3,...,3},ytick={-4,-3,...,5},grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-2.5:2,samples=2]{2*x+1} node[pos=0.9,right,text=blue!70!black]{$y=2x+1$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The graph of $y=2x+1$ is shown. Use it to solve $2x+1=-3$.

options.why: Ignored the negative sign on the target and read across from $y=3$ instead of $y=-3$.
Read the $y$-value $-3$ instead of the $x$-coordinate at the intersection.
Read the intersection one gridline too high, at $(-1,-1)$.

questions.solution_text:
Draw the horizontal line $y=-3$.
It meets $y=2x+1$ at $(-2,-3)$.
$x=-2$

questions.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[width=6cm,height=6cm,axis lines=middle,axis line style={-{Stealth}},xmin=-1,xmax=4,ymin=-4,ymax=3,xtick={-1,0,...,4},ytick={-4,-3,...,3},minor x tick num=1,grid=both,clip=false,scaled ticks=false,ticklabel style={/pgf/number format/fixed},xlabel={$x$},ylabel={$y$}]\addplot[thick,blue!70!black,<->,domain=-0.5:3,samples=2]{2*x-3} node[pos=0.1,left,text=blue!70!black]{$y=2x-3$};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The graph of $y=2x-3$ is shown. Use it to solve $2x-3=2$.

options.why: Rounded the intersection up to the nearest whole gridline instead of reading $x=2.5$.
Read the lower gridline $x=2$ instead of interpolating to the half-gridline.

questions.solution_text:
Draw the horizontal line $y=2$.
It meets $y=2x-3$ between $x=2$ and $x=3$, at $(2.5,2)$.
$x=2.5$
